paper_title: AI translation of literary texts is "fine", but readers still prefer human translations

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: AI translation of literary works is increasingly common. While the content may be rendered adequately, we do not know enough about how readers experience it in terms of immersiveness and literary effect, aspects poorly captured by automatic machine translation metrics or human evaluation targeting fluency and adequacy. We ask 15 avid readers to compare recently published human translations (HT) to machine translations (MT) generated with an agentic large language model (LLM)-based pipeline, for 15 recent novels in French, Polish, and Japanese and translated into English. Readers evaluated approximately 8K-word excerpts in two conditions: immersive reading of the whole excerpt (30 comparisons) and close reading of 386 aligned HT-MT chunk pairs (772 comparisons), with two readers per book and in alternating order of presentation. Overall, readers find MT "fine", but prefer HT (slightly at excerpt-level 19/30, more clearly at chunk-level 522/772) for its ease, clarity, and immersive nature. Readers' highlights show that MT's quality varies more within one book than HT's does. Crucially, readers cannot reliably tell the two apart (17/30 guess correctly) and tend to prefer the version they believe to be human. Automatic metrics, including LLM-as-a-judge approaches, fail to recover reader preferences and favor MT. We release LAIT (Literary AI Translation), a reader-centered evaluation dataset with 1K reader comments, 2K judgments and preference ratings, and 7.2K span-level annotations, along with our evaluation protocol and supporting interface.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Machine-translated literature is reaching real readership.1 Veen Bosch & Keuning, the largest Dutch trade publisher, announced AI-assisted translation (Creamer, 2024); Shogakukan, Japanese publisher, released an app for the US and Canadian markets featuring AI-assisted translation of light novels (Nishioka, 2024); Amazon introduced Kindle Translate for self-publishing authors (Amazon Staff, 2025); and services such as GlobeScribe market AI translation to smaller publishers and selfpublished authors (GlobeScribe.AI Ltd). reader picking up novel translated in 2026 may be confronted with AI translation even if they are not aware of it. This raises interesting questions: If AI-translated literature is reaching readers, how does it change their reading experience and perception of the authors work compared to professional human translation? Readers respond to rhythm, emotions, voice, and deliberate word choices (Brems and Ramos Pinto, 2013; Walker, 2021), but this experience cannot be captured by current evaluation setups which often concentrate on small units (Karpinska and Iyyer, 2023; Wu et al., 2025; Zhang et al., 2025b) and break the immersion with segment-level ratings (Kocmi et al., 2025) or error annotations (Freitag et al., 2021). To capture the value of machine literary translation, we need to pose this question directly to the reader (Carpuat et al., 2025). To fill this gap, we recruit 15 avid readers and ask them to read and compare 8K-word-long excerpts translated from Japanese, Polish, and French into English2 by professional literary translators (HT) and an agentic LLM-based pipeline (MT), reading each version in full and uninterrupted, as they would published book (immersive reading). Then, we ask them to look closer at both texts in 300-word chunks presented side by side and to highlight phrasing they judge as good or poor beterm readers are more familiar with. 1We use AI translation and MT interchangeably for LLMbased translation; questionnaires used AI as it is likely the 2We choose new books translated recently (20252026) into English to mitigate the training data contamination issue. 1 Figure 1: Evaluation pipeline: Avid readers of published fiction evaluate two versions of an 8,000-word book excerpt: human translation ( HT ) and an AI-generated machine translation ( MT ). Participants (1) read the first translation, (2) complete perception questionnaire, (3) read the competing translation, (4) complete second questionnaire, (5) compare both versions, (6) take one-day break, and (7) perform side-by-side evaluation of 300-word chunks, selecting the preferred version with justification and an indication of whether the choice was difficult. The presentation order is counterbalanced: each book is evaluated in both HT-first and MT-first conditions, and each reader evaluates two different books, encountering HT first for one and MT first for the other. fore indicating which version they prefer (close reading). Crucially, besides ratings, preference choices, and chunk-level highlights, we also ask the readers to explicitly justify why they prefer given version, indicate the strength of their preferences, and why they suspect text is MT (Figure 1). Overall, readers show slight but consistent preference for HT, which is clearer under close reading (522/772) than under immersive reading (19/30). However, readers do not completely reject MT as they often find it readable and sometimes preferred. Notably, chunk-level span annotations show that MT quality varies much more within single book than HT, and its overall success depends more on the book than the source language. Reader comments suggest that HT is praised more for immersion, naturalness, and literary rendering (e.g., having soul (HT) vs. translation for dummies (MT)). MT sometimes wins due to readability or local word choices. Readers also struggle to identify MT reliably. Their accuracy remains close to chance even after reading both excerpts (17/30). Their explanations show that they are misled by common AI tells (Russell et al., 2025), such as em-dashes or assumptions about AI not using swear words. Finally, automatic metrics, including LLM-as-a-judge approaches, fail to recover reader preferences and favor MTs. To summarize, our contributions are threefold: @ DATA: We introduce LAIT, readerannotated dataset of 15 FR/PL/JAEN novel openings (20252026), each aligned across source, published HT, and agentic MT at the excerpt, chunk, and paragraph levels and evaluated by two readers, yielding 2K ratings and preferences, 1K comments, and 7.2K spanlevel annotations. LAIT additionally provides an unannotated 16-book development set with 1.7K paragraph-level alignments across five candidate MT systems. (cid:219) ANALYSIS: We show that modern literary MT into English can be readable and hard to detect, but its quality varies more within book than HT, and readers still value HT. (cid:135) METHODOLOGY: We introduce readercentered evaluation protocol combining immersive and close reading, and release our guidelines and annotation software to support reproducibility and future work.

sections.title: 2 Data & Methods
sections.content: We study literary translation from French, Polish, and Japanese into English by comparing professional human translations (HT) with machine translations (MT) of the same fiction excerpts. We construct LAIT, corpus of 31 book-opening excerpts (8K words each) from recently published novels: 15 for the human evaluation and 16 for MT-pipeline 2 selection (2.1). Each source excerpt was paired with its published HT and corresponding MT. Our human evaluation (2.2) combined immersive reading of whole excerpts with close reading of aligned 300-word chunks, comparing HT and MT both as extended reading experiences and as sets of specific translation choices.

sections.title: LAIT
sections.content: Book selection. We selected recently published, critically acclaimed fiction books originally written in French, Polish, and Japanese, with professional English translations published in 20252026, to mitigate data contamination (Jacovi et al., 2023; Kocyigit et al., 2025). Overall, we obtained 15 books for human evaluation,3 with five books per source language (see Table 1), and reserved 16 books for selecting the MT pipeline, for total of 31 books (see for the full book list).4 Source and target languages. We selected French, Polish, and Japanese as source languages because they span different language families, cultures, and literary traditions. We chose English as the target language to probe the upper bound of translation capability, since modern language models are exceptionally strong in English (Ahuja et al., 2023; Zhu et al., 2024). Furthermore, the researchers familiarity with all the languages facilitated data collection and analysis. Excerpt extraction. For each book, we selected an opening excerpt from the English human translation (HT) of 8K words (µ = 7,623, SD = 449) under whitespace tokenization while preserving paragraph boundaries. This length supported immersive reading while keeping the task feasible for readers evaluation. We then manually identified the corresponding source excerpt to obtain sourcetarget mapping with HT. Preprocessing. Before generating MTs, we manually inspected the excerpts and removed editorial notes and footnotes while preserving the original paragraph structure. Additional normalization, e.g., unifying quotation marks, was applied to MTs and HTs before evaluation to minimize the influence of surface-level features (see for details). 3We limit our human evaluation to 15 books due to the prohibitive cost. 4All source books and translations were purchased and processed solely for research purposes. To comply with Fair Dealing guidelines for research, we have retained no more than 10% of any individual work. HT MT

Words are whitespace-delimited counts. Tokens are computed with tiktoken (o200k_base). For SRC , we report only token counts, since Japanese cannot be split on whitespaces. MT pipeline selection. To select strong MT pipeline for the evaluation, we compared five configurations on 16-book development set: two prompting pipelines, P1 full-excerpt translation with post-editing and P2 chunk-level translation with excerpt-level post-editing, each using either GPT-5.4 or Gemini 3.1 Pro, and P3, an AutoFictioninspired agentic pipeline (Pham et al., 2026) using Claude Code and Codex.5 We used blind five-way preference task: six raters6 each assessed 23 books. For each book, raters read five anonymized translations of the same excerpt presented in random order, selected the version they most preferred as literary translation, and provided short comment. The configurations were closely matched, with one exception: P3 was chosen most often, but only by small margin, while P1 with GPT-5.4 was the clear outlier.7 We adopted P3, treating this as practical pipeline choice, not because of evidence that it is decisively better. See for details. Agentic pipeline. We adapted the AutoFiction long-form fiction generation framework (Pham et al., 2026) to literary translation. (cid:212) Preprocessing: Claude Code analyzed the source excerpt to produce translation guidelines (e.g., register, terminology, translation memories). The source was then divided into 1K-token chunks while preserving paragraphs. 2. Chunk-level: Codex translated each chunk, which was then reviewed along two dimensions: (1) translation quality (e.g., faithfulness) using modified version of LiTransProQA (Zhang et al., 2025b) (Codex), and (2) literary quality review (e.g., voice, style) adapted from AutoFiction (Claude Code). Based on the output of these reviews, an acceptance gate9 determined whether the chunk proceeded to the next stage or was sent for revision. We allowed up to three local cycles total due to the high associated cost and small gains. 3. _ Excerpt-level: Revised chunks were merged into full draft and reviewed for (1) 8We set the reasoning effort to the maximum value, which is max for Claude Code and xhigh for Codex. Both agents were accessed via monthly subscription at the cost of $400 USD for the entire experiment. 9The gate uses the review rubrics described in C; chunks pass only when the automatic reviewers find no major faithfulness or literary-quality problems. global literary quality and (2) cross-chunk consistency (Codex). The results passed through final acceptance gate, which triggered revision by Claude Code if minimum quality thresholds were not met.10 We allowed up to two global cycles. Chunking. To prepare the data for the chunklevel close reading, we split each HT excerpt into 300-word chunks (µ = 296, SD = 49) preserving the paragraph boundaries, yielding 386 chunks across the 15 evaluation books. We then aligned these chunks using Gemini 3.1 Pro to the corresponding chunks from MT and source text. All alignments were verified manually.

sections.title: 2.2 Human Evaluation Protocol
sections.content: Overview. We evaluated 15 long book excerpts (8K words) translated by professional human translators (published HT) and by our pipeline (agentic MT). Each excerpt was evaluated by two readers and each reader evaluated two books11 with total of 60 excerpt-level evaluations (immersive reading, 15 books 2 readers 2 translations). After one-day break, each reader evaluated the 10The final gate uses the excerpt-level rubrics described in C; drafts pass only when the automatic reviewers find no major global-quality or cross-chunk consistency problems. 11The evaluations for both books are consecutive, i.e., the reader has to finish one book before proceeding to the next.

Participants first read one translation of the entire excerpt and answered eight single-reading questions about it, with access only to that version. They then read the second translation and answered the same eight questions, again with access only to that version. Only after completing both readings did they compare the two translations directly and answer seven comparative questions. Reading order was counterbalanced: for each book, one participant read HT first and the other read MT first; each participant also read HT first for one of their two assigned books and MT first for the other. Participants were not told which version was MT. Close reading. After one-day break, participants completed side-by-side close reading task on shorter chunks of about 300 words with HT and MT aligned. For each chunk, they highlighted wording they judged especially good Figure 3: Study design and evaluation counts. Two readers evaluated each of the 15 book excerpts (30 bookreader evaluations). same excerpt at the chunk level, with 300-word MT and HT chunks presented side by side for total of 772 comparisons (close reading; 386 aligned HTMT chunk pairs, each judged by both readers). Figure 3 summarizes the study design and evaluation counts. See Figure 1 for the pipeline. Participants. We recruited 15 avid readers through Upwork12 (reported reading 12120 books/year). All readers primarily read in English and reported being very comfortable reading long literary texts in English. They evaluated each excerpt as target readers, i.e., the English readership which the published translations target. Each reader evaluated two book excerpts, and for each excerpt evaluated both MT and HT (within-subjects design). We aimed to accommodate reading preferences by matching each reader to their preferred genre/story type. Participants were paid $110 USD per book with $510 USD bonus upon completion. The task took approximately 4h per book at an hourly rate of $27.50 USD. Total cost of the evaluation including bonuses and Upwork fees was $4K USD. The protocol was approved by the Institutional Review Board. See for details. Procedure. Prior to the task, each participant provided informed consent, filled in demographic survey on their AI use and reading habits, and reviewed interface guidelines.13 They were then provided with the credentials to the interface where they completed an immersive reading task followed by one-day break and close reading task. Both tasks were administered at an interface designed to mimic book readers. The close reading interface remained locked for 24h after completing the immersive reading stage, enforcing the one-day break. After finishing both assigned books, participants completed post-study questionnaire. 12upwork.com 13The exact wording can be found in D. 5 or poor, chose the translation they preferred, explained their choice, and indicated whether this decision was difficult. Questionnaires. We used four questionnaires. The immersive reading task included two: (1) single-reading questionnaire presented immediately after each reading, and (2) comparative questionnaire presented after both readings. Together, these captured global reading experience. The close reading task used (3) questionnaire for each chunk pair, capturing local preference. Finally, after participants completed both books, we administered (4) post-study questionnaire with four open-ended questions about their thoughts on translations. See Table 2 for questions. Quality control. To ensure data quality, we required participants to submit passphrase embedded in the guidelines before they received their credentials, confirming they had read the instructions. We also recorded screen-reading time and detected anomalies such as fast scrolling.14 Finally, we ran the Pangram AI detector (Emi and Spero, 2024) over participant comments to flag potentially AI-generated text.

sections.title: 3 Results & Analysis
sections.content: In this section, we present the results of our evaluation. Overall, we find slight but consistent preference for HT, though readers often find MT readable and sometimes even prefer it. One recurring observation is that both HT and MT vary in quality, but MT varies morein our translations into English, human translations tend to be relatively stable across novel, whereas MT quality can fluctuate more from chunk to chunk within single book. We also find that readers cannot reliably distinguish MT from HT, often falling for AI tells (e.g., mistaking em-dashes for sign of MT). We further analyze reader comments and post-study observations, and present preliminary results for translations into Japanese, Polish, French, and Spanish. Finally, we examine how automatic metrics correlate with human judgments and how machine translations from different systems compare to the human translations. 14Two readers were excluded for insufficient comment detail and fast reading, and replaced by two others to keep the sample size at 15. 15No comments were flagged as AI-generated or AIassisted. Figure 4: The distribution of readers preferences between the human translation ( HT ) and the machine translation ( MT ) after excerpt-level immersive reading and chunk-level close reading. The readers were judging which version they preferred and how strong the preference was. Figure 5: The distribution of readers ratings after immersive reading of each excerpt in isolation. The readers were judging acceptability (whether it is acceptable as published translation), smoothness, immersiveness (whether it supported immersion), and continue (whether they would continue reading).

sections.title: 3.1 Readers’ ratings and preferences
sections.content: In this section, we analyze readers preference choices (MT vs. HT), their ratings of each version (e.g., smoothness, acceptability), and the spans they highlighted as well or poorly worded, reporting preferences at both the excerpt and chunk levels. Readers prefer HT, especially in close reading. After reading both excerpt-level translations, participants chose which version they would prefer to continue reading (co-Q3). Across the 30 excerptlevel judgments, 19 favored HT and 11 favored MT, with 16 clear preferences for HT compared to 7 clear preferences for MT (Figure 4). The preference for HT was not statistically significant (orderadjusted HT probability = 63.4%, p=.148).16 16Unless otherwise stated, statistical tests in this section use mixed-effects regression models with random intercepts for reader and book when estimable and model family appropriate for the response variable type. When mixed-effects model is singular, we report the corresponding simpler model. See for details. 6 HT is preferred on dialogue and word choice. At the excerpt level, readers also compared the two versions on dialogue and word choice (Figure 6). On both attributes, HT was preferred more often than MT, although the differences were not statistically significant. For dialogue, 17 judgments favored HT, 9 favored MT, and 4 found no meaningful difference (order-adjusted signed preference mean = 0.27, p=.109); for word choice, 18 favored HT, 10 favored MT, and 2 found no meaningful difference (order-adjusted signed preference mean = 0.27, p=.131). HT preference does not imply MT failure. The overall preference for HT does not mean that readers rejected MT across the board. Of the 11 excerptlevel judgments that favored MT, 7 expressed clear preference (Figure 4). After immersive reading, 54% of MT responses indicated moderate to strong willingness to continue reading (a rating of 4 or 5), compared with 66% for HT (Figure 5). And in the close reading, roughly one-third (32%) of chunk-level choices favored MT. MT preference varies by book but not by language. The one-third MT share in close reading was stable across source languages (French 32%, Japanese 34%, and Polish 31%).18 By contrast, preference toward MT varied across books, from 4% to 88%, and book identity was strongly associated with chunk-level preference (p<.001). The clearest case is Hooked: Novel of Obsession, preferred in MT 88% of the time and consistently by both readers, suggesting that sometimes HT may fall short. Readers highlight more positive evidence in HT and more negative evidence in MT. The span annotations show the same pattern as the preference judgments. In close reading, readers highlighted more positive evidence in HT than in MT (107.8 vs. 68.5 highlighted words per 1K words), while they highlighted more negative evidence in MT than in HT (100.7 vs. 42.9 highlighted words per 1K words; Figure 7). Overall, HT received significantly more positive evidence spans than MT (rate ratio = 1.69, p<.001), and significantly fewer negative evidence spans than MT (rate ratio = 0.43, p<.001; Figure 9). The higher rate of negative evidence for MT holds across French, Japanese, Figure 6: The distribution of readers excerpt-level preferences for dialogue handling and word choice after reading both the human translation ( HT ) and the machine translation ( MT ). The readers were judging which version handled dialogue better overall and which version had more varied or expressive word choice. Preference for HT is clearer in close reading (ch-Q2) with 522 of 772 chunk-level judgments favoring HT, including 205 strong preferences, compared to 250 judgments favoring MT, including 54 strong preferences (Figure 4). The chunk-level HT preference was significant (order-adjusted HT probability = 76.2%, p=.011), and remained significant when preference strength was modeled directly (p=.010).17 HT is rated higher for quality, especially for smoothness and as publishable translation. After reading each version, participants rated it on 5-point scale for acceptability as published translation, smoothness, support for immersion, and willingness to continue reading (Figure 5). HT received large proportion of top ratings (4 or 5) and no rating of 1. Statistical analysis shows that HT had significantly higher odds of receiving better ratings for acceptability as published translation and for smoothness. Compared with MT, HT had 4.0 times the odds of higher acceptability rating (p=.0069) and 4.3 times the odds of higher smoothness rating (p=.0029). The differences for immersion and willingness to continue reading were smaller and not statistically significant, but still favored HT. 17Reading order (HT-first vs. MT-first) was included as predictor in the preference models and was not significant (excerpt-level: p=.705, chunk-level: p=.536). 18There was no evidence that language predicted MT preference after accounting for reading order and repeated judgments by reader and book (p=.928). 7 Figure 7: Distribution of good and poor span highlights by source language and translation type. Figure 8: Share of close-reading chunks with dense poor-span evidence. For each threshold, the curve shows the percentage of HT and MT chunks with at least that many poor-highlighted words per 1K words. MT has substantially more high-density poor-span chunks. and Polish, with MT receiving 97.9, 116.1, and 88.3 negative-highlighted words per 1K words, respectively, compared with 31.1, 59.2, and 39.0 for HT. Negative evidence is also more concentrated in MT chunks, with 41.7% of them having at least 100 negative-highlighted words per 1K words compared to only 11.9% of HT chunks (Figure 8). Figure 10 (upper row) shows an example where both readers preferred HT along with their comments and highlights. Span annotations closely track explicit preferences. Highlighted spans strongly align with readers explicit chunk preferences. For each chunk judgment, we computed net span score for each version, subtracting the amount of text marked as poor from the amount marked as good. In non-tied cases, the version with the higher net highlightedword score matched the readers stated preference in 691 of 755 judgments (91.5%, exact binomial p<.001). Using span counts rather than highlightedword counts yielded an even stronger alignment with 692 of 722 judgments matching the stated preference (95.8%, exact binomial p<.001). This suggests that span annotations directly capture the local textual evidence driving reader preferences. Both MT and HT vary in quality, but MT has more variable weak spots. We see this most clearly in the chunk-level annotations. For each book, we aggregate readers good and poor highlights by chunk and compare how much the HT and MT versions fluctuate across chunks. Poor wording highlights were more variable for MT than HT in 13 of 15 books, both when measured as the number of poor wording highlights per 1K words and as poor-highlighted words per 1K words (p=.0074; paired sign test across books). We further look at net quality, i.e., good minus poor highlight counts per 1K words. This analysis shows similar pattern, with MT being more variable in 12 out of 15 books (p=.0352).

sections.content: ratings quality excerpt-level smoothness, (i.e., acceptability, immersion, and continue_reading) show similar tendency. MT ratings span the full 1 to 5 scale, whereas HT is never rated 1. Within participants, MT ratings also vary slightly more across the two excerpts each reader saw than HT ratings do (mean absolute difference of 1.30 vs. 1.05 points on the 5-point scale), although this within-participant difference is not statistically significant (p=.285). Together, these results suggest that MT is less stable in its weak spots, and while readers sometimes find MT smooth or readable, they also encounter chunks (or entire excerpts) with more visible problems. Literary preference has shared and subjective components. Because every excerpt was evaluated by two readers, we further look at interreader agreement in two ways. Specifically, we look at whether paired readers chose the same translation as better on the excerptand chunklevels, and whether they assigned similar quality ratings to given translation. For direct HT vs. MT choices, agreement increased for the local judgments. Whole-excerpt preference was the most subjective case, where readers chose the same version in only 6 of 15 books (40.0%; AC1 = 0.120). This was higher for chunk-level choices (63.7%; AC1 = 0.355), and even more so once the chunks marked as similar quality were excluded (72.2%; AC1 = 0.546), with the highest agreement when both readers marked one version as significantly better (79.5%; AC1 = 0.667). The gap widened for more literary and experiential qualities: HT received more praise for engagement/immersion (12/30 vs. 5/30), and description/vividness was among its top labels (9/30). For single-reading quality ratings, overall agreement across acceptability, smoothness, immersion, and continue_reading was moderate (AC2 = 0.426). It was stronger for HT than for MT (AC2 = 0.616 vs. 0.216), and within each version clearest for concrete craft ratings, e.g., HT acceptability and smoothness (0.765 and 0.725), and weakest for experiential ones on MT, i.e., immersion and continue_reading (0.057 and 0.089) 20. Together, these results suggest that readers agree more on textual quality, especially when looking locally, but diverge more when deciding how much an uneven text still functions for them as literature. Because each reader compares HT and MT directly (i.e., within-subject design), this disagreement is less likely to reflect differences in how readers use the rating scale than genuine variation in literary preference. Hence, we view the disagreement as an integral part of literary judgment, combining shared sensitivity to textual quality with reader-specific priorities (Plank, 2022; Guerberof-Arenas and Toral, 2024; Marco et al., 2025).

sections.title: 3.3 Can readers detect machine translation?
sections.content: Readers cannot reliably tell MT from HT. In the single-reading questionnaire, readers correctly labeled the MT excerpt as machine-translated in 16 of 30 cases and the HT excerpt as human-translated in 18 of 30 cases. Overall, we do not find any evidence that the readers were able to distinguish MT from HT in single-reading above the chance level (34/60, p=.413) with no difference between the MT and HT accuracy (p=.557). Direct comparison did not change this trend. Even after seeing both translations, readers selected the actual MT version as more likely to be AItranslated only in 17 out of 30 pairs (p=.585; Figure 13). While correct guesses were more confident after seeing both translations, that increase was not significant (p=.129). Moreover, the high confidence did not guarantee correctness. After reading both excerpts, 8 of the 20 very or extremely confident guesses were wrong (40%), including 1 of 8 extremely confident guesses (12.5%). See for more details. In this section, we explore whether the readers were able to detect which text was machine-translated. Readers prefer the translation they perceive as human. Readers final MT guess was connected 11 Figure 11: The most frequent labels in readers comments about the human translation ( HT ) and the machine translation ( MT ). Rows separate single-reading praise, single-reading criticism, excerpt-level preference reasons, and chunk-level preference reasons. Comments could receive multiple labels. Figure 13: Machine-translation (MT) identification accuracy and confidence in the single-reading questionnaire (n=60) and comparative questionnaire (n=30). Colors represent confidence levels, from not at all to extremely confident. them that this translation, despite being better, is MT. What cues do readers use to identify MT? Word choice was the most frequently cited cue, (9/13) for both correct guesses (Figure 12), making it ultimately nondiscriminative. Correct guesses described wording as awkward, incorrect ones as generic or weird (people just dont speak like that). (8/17) and incorrect What was more telling was whether the readers relied on concrete textual evidence or on folk theories about AI (i.e., the common idea of what AI writing is). The strongest separator was formatting (M4) such as scene dividers or paragraph breaks, which made it hard to follow. Another cue with Figure 12: The cues readers mentioned when explaining which translation they believed was machine-translated ( MT ). Correct judgments identify the actual MT version; incorrect judgments identify the human translation ( HT ) as MT . Comments could receive multiple cue labels. to their preferences; in 28 of 30 comparisons they judged their preferred translation to be the human one (all 11 readers who preferred MT believed it was human, as did 17 of 19 who preferred HT). Only in two cases did the readers prefer HT while still judging it to be MT. In one case the reader was misled by em-dashes (i.e., strong belief that AI uses more em-dashes). In another one the reader noticed French structure in the HT for book that was originally written in French, which convinced 12 stronger discriminative power was literal rendering (M1; 6/17 vs. 3/13), where MT was described as reading like literal dictionary translation or with the conversion of French sentence structure, along with poor sentence flow or grammar (M3; 7/17 vs. 4/13, e.g., run-on and cut-off sentences). The most deceptive were folk theories, which resulted in accuracy no better than chance (M10; 5/17 vs. 5/13). These include em-dashes perceived as classic sign of AI, or belief that AI prefers to avoid swearing (Russell et al., 2025). Least reliable were references to subjective literary quality, i.e., voice and emotional tone (M7) was cited mostly by readers who guessed wrong (2/17 vs. 5/13), who called the human translation flat and mechanical and so took it for the machine. Kendall τ (Kendall, 1938) correlation with human judgments at chunklevel is weak to negative for all metric variants (LITRANSPROQA τ = 0.124, METRICX-QE τ = 0.075, COMETKIWI PARAGRAPH MEAN τ = 0.318, METRICX-QE PARAGRAPH MEAN τ = 0.216), and is identical whether computed over all judgments or only those where readers agree, since clear human agreement usually favors HT while metrics favor MT. This result is consistent with prior work reporting that metrics often fail to recover human preferences for literary machine translation(Thai et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2025b,a).

sections.title: 3.4 Can automatic metrics capture readers’
sections.content: languages other than English? preferences? In this section, we explore how well automatic metrics can capture readers preferences for the chunk-level judgments. Setup. We compare chunk-level human judgments with automatic metrics. Since several metrics are constrained by context-window size, we use the par3 aligner (Thai et al., 2022) to obtain paragraph-level sourceHTMT alignments for the evaluation data. We then score the paragraphs and map the scores to chunks containing these paragraphs. chunk-level score was computed by taking the average of paragraph-level scores within each chunk. See for details. Evaluation methods. We test the three most popular evaluation methods: METRICX-QE (Juraska et al., 2024), COMETKIWI (Rei et al., 2022), and LITRANSPROQA (Zhang et al., 2025b) with Gemini 3.1 Pro (Google DeepMind, 2026) as judge. We score the paragraphs directly with METRICXQE and LITRANSPROQA as they both can handle chunk length. We additionally score the paragraphs and then compute the average chunk score with COMETKIWI and METRICX-QE.25 Can the difference between MT and HT be captured with automatic metrics? Across the closereading chunks, all automatic metric variants prefer MT over HT (LITRANSPROQA MT: 0.996, HT: 0.920; 25We compute METRICX-QE over both chunk and paragraph as it is likely better adapted to shorter lengths. For LITRANSPROQA we report only chunk-level evaluation due to the cost and since the model can handle 300-word texts. In this section, we present results of small scale case study of translations into Spanish, French, Polish, and Japanese in order to understand the gap between these languages and English. Setup. In order to explore whether the strong performance of MT holds beyond English, we run small multilingual case study in which readers compared HT and MT into Spanish, French, Polish, and Japanese.26 We translate My Grandfather, the Master Detective (Japanese source) into Spanish, French, and Polish, and The Witcher: Crossroads of Ravens (Polish source) into Japanese, choosing these two books because both have recently been professionally translated and published in the respective target languages. HT is strongly preferred for translations into languages other than English. The HT vs. MT contrast was much sharper than in translation into English. At the chunk level, all readers preferred HT (103/111 chunks, 92.8%), and at the excerpt level only the French reader chose MT, however, they still rated HT high in single-reading with just one point difference between the MT and HT (5 vs. 4 for acceptability, smoothness, etc.). By comparison, the remaining four readers preferred HT, with single-reading ratings favoring HT most for translation into Japanese and Polish. Importantly, even the French reader changed their preferences 26For Spanish, French, and Polish the readers were researchers and volunteers who are native speakers of the given language; we used two readers for the Polish book. For Japanese we followed the English procedure and hired one reader via Upwork, paid $110 USD. towards HT in the close-reading task. More details are reported in F. Readers identified MT through concrete translationese. Unlike in the English-facing study, where MT origin was difficult to detect, for translations into other languages the detection accuracy was better with 4 out of 5 readers identifying MT correctly and with strong average confidence (mean 4.4/5). Furthermore, the readers explanations point to concrete issues such as translationese. One Polish reader states they were 100% sure that the MT cannot possibly be human-translated (...) [at] around 10% of reading. Readers flagged grammatical and structural problems, such as sentences hanging there as if directly translated from the source, with no predicate, as well as source language calques, including the third person for Kaede when she speaks about herself.27 One recurring cue was also vocabulary, especially in cases where MT failed to use standard term and instead supplied description like student who has not mastered the vocabulary fully yet. For example, where the human translator in Polish wrote nekrolog (obituary), the MT wrote prasowe artykuły posmiertne (posthumous press articles). Other times MT used wrong or nonidiomatic term, for instance, in Spanish, librerías de viejo where the human wrote librerías de segunda mano (second-hand bookshops), phrasing the reader judged not idiomatic in any variety of Spanish.

sections.title: 4 Related Work
sections.content: We build on prior work exploring the evaluation of literary machine translation. Automatic evaluation. Automatic evaluation is practical but surface-level (Papineni et al., 2002; Popovic, 2015), while trained neural metrics (Rei et al., 2020, 2022; Guerreiro et al., 2024; Juraska et al., 2023, 2024) were shown to work poorly on literary machine translation (Thai et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2025b) and offer weak signal for what humans perceive as literary quality (Gerrits et al., 2026). LLM-as-a-judge approaches based on question answering protocols were developed but tested only on short passages (Zhang et al., 2025b; Shafayat et al., 2024) and it is unclear how they handle longer dependencies. Recent document-level 27It is common in Japanese to refer to yourself in the third person but in other languages it often seems either childish or weird. and long-document MT work often combines automatic metrics with LLM-as-a-judge evaluation (Läubli et al., 2018, 2020; Wang et al., 2026), but such evaluations still do not directly measure reader experience. Human evaluation. Human evaluation often relies on error annotations (Lommel et al., 2013; Freitag et al., 2021; Kocmi et al., 2024) or crowdsourced ratings (Graham et al., 2013; Freitag et al., 2021), which do not capture reading experience (Zhang et al., 2025a). Most evaluation of literary MT uses short passages or local judgments (Wu et al., 2025; Karpinska and Iyyer, 2023; Wang et al., 2023; Kocmi et al., 2025), missing the purpose of literary translation as sustained reading. Prior work on literary MT and reception remains narrower than our reader-facing comparison of professional HT and recent MT over long fiction excerpts (Guerberof-Arenas and Toral, 2020, 2022, 2024; Gerrits and Guerberof-Arenas, 2025; Moorkens et al., 2018; Castaldo et al., 2025; Matusov, 2019; Taivalkoski-Shilov, 2019b; Webster et al., 2020; Van Egdom et al., 2023). This reader-facing framing also relates to narrative transportation, AIperception, literary translator voice, and ethics in machine-assisted literary translation (Green and Brock, 2000; Porter and Machery, 2024; Zhu et al., 2025; Kenny and Winters, 2020; TaivalkoskiShilov, 2019a). In contrast, we target readers experience of long literary MT versus HT in immersive reading, and what issues they notice in close reading.

sections.title: 5 Discussion & Conclusion
sections.content: We evaluated literary MT in immersive and close reading by comparing professional human translations (HT) with strong agentic LLM pipeline (MT) across 15 recent French, Polish, and Japanese novels translated into English. Our analysis reveals that readers found MT reasonable and were not able to detect it reliably, but they consistently preferred HT, especially in the close reading. Preferences varied mostly by book, not source language, and readers often preferred the version they believed to be human, while automatic metrics favored MT. We release LAIT and our evaluation pipeline to support future research on literary MT.

sections.title: Limitations
sections.content: Excerpt scope. Due to financial limitations we translate and evaluate translations of novel open14 ings, not full books. Literary quality, pacing, and narrative voice may evolve over longer reading, and book-spanning context and background may be lost by LLM translations of full books. These factors may impact judgments over longer translations, however we note that 8K words is already at the length of short stories. Corpus selection. We selected recently published (20252026), critically acclaimed fiction with professional English translations available to mitigate training-data contamination. However, this limits the diversity of our books and may not represent the whole set of literature out there. Languages and directions. The main evaluation is French, Polish, and Japanese into English only (n=5 books per source language). Our multilingual case study covers two additional books and four target languages with much smaller reader sample and mixed recruitment (Upwork vs. researchers/volunteers); those results are exploratory and should not be generalized. much larger-scale study is required to determine whether the effects observed in the current work apply to other sourcetarget language pairs. Sample size and agreement. Each book is read by only two readers recruited via Upwork. This scale was necessary for immersive, long-form evaluation to be financially feasible, but limits statistical power and leaves chunk-level inter-reader agreement modest in places. We employ withinsubject design, where each reader judges both MT and HT for the same excerpts, reducing individual variability and requiring fewer annotators than between-subjects design for statistical analysis (Allen, 2017).

sections.title: Ethics Statement
sections.content: Human participants. This study was approved by the Simon Fraser University Research Ethics Board (#30003790). Participants gave informed consent before the evaluation (D), could withdraw with prorated compensation, and were paid fairly for the amount and difficulty of work ($110 USD per book, $27.50/hour for 4 hours of work), plus completion bonuses. Participant identities are anonymized by participant codes. we retain no more than 10% of any individual work. The dataset will be released for research purposes only. Corpus and societal impact. Our corpus overrepresents internationally published, commercially successful fiction. We study AI-assisted literary translation to inform evaluation practice and publishing transparency: not to advocate replacing human translators or deceiving readers; in fact, we demonstrate that readers often prefer humanauthored translations. As LLM-translated fiction enters trade and self-publishing channels, we hope that better reader-centered evaluation can support fair labor practices and help readers make informed choices. Risks of literary machine translation. Literary MT can look acceptable while still changing the reading experience. As our reader comments show, problems with phrasing, context, and voice can affect the readers impression of book and its author, especially when AI-translated fiction reaches readers with little human review. Like other LLM output, MT may also carry biased or harmful wording into the translation. AI use disclosure. We utilize coding agents to produce plots and edit table layout. We further use language models as writing assistants but no part of this paper was generated from scratch.

sections.title: A Dataset
sections.content: In this section of the appendix, we provide additional information regarding our dataset. Table 4 lists the evaluation books used in the English human evaluation and the development books used to choose an optimal MT pipeline. We report the original title, author, title of the translation, translator, original publication date, and translation publication date. Detailed summary statistics for the evaluation books, aligned chunks, including collected free-form comments are reported in Table 5.

sections.title: B Machine Translation
sections.content: In this section of the appendix, we provide more details on our machine translation pipeline, including the selection of reasonable translation approach. Machine translation pipelines. Overall, we experiment with three pipelines in five setups: P1 Full-excerpt translation with post-editing by (a) GPT-5.4 or (b) Gemini 3.1 Pro. P2 Chunk-level translation with excerpt-level post-editing by (a) GPT-5.4 or (b) Gemini 3.1 Pro. P3 An agentic pipeline with Claude Code (Opus 4.6) and Codex (GPT-5.4), inspired by AutoFiction (Pham et al., 2026), an agent-based novel-generation pipeline. We select GPT-5.4 and Gemini 3.1 Pro because these model families were strong performers in the WMT 2025 General Translation Shared Task (Kocmi et al., 2025). We further explore coding agents because they are optimized for longcontext tasks and for handling multiple reference documents. Prompts. We deploy different prompts for each pipeline. As the pipelines include multiple long prompts we refrain from including the entire prompt texts in the appendix and make them accessible through the repository instead (github.com/ Yves575/lait). Details on prompting pipelines. P1 and P2 used the same two-stage prompting procedure. In the first stage, the model was prompted as an expert literary translator and asked to translate the source excerpt into English while preserving meaning, tone, voice, register, rhythm, stylistic effect, ambiguity, characterization, and differences between narrator and character voices. The prompt also instructed the model to avoid unjustified omissions or additions and to output only the translation. In the second stage, the model received the original source excerpt together with its own first-pass English translation and was tasked with revision. [P1] Full-excerpt translation. For P1, each complete source excerpt was translated in single firstpass call. This condition therefore gave the model the entire opening excerpt at once, including all available discourse context, recurring names, register shifts, and dialogue patterns. The resulting complete English draft was then passed to the postediting stage together with the complete source excerpt. The post-editing pass was also performed at the excerpt level, so both stages in P1 had access to the full source span. [P2] Chunk-level translation with excerpt-level post-editing. For P2, the first-pass translation was performed over smaller source chunks. We counted tokens with the tiktoken o200k_base encoding and targeted maximum of 1K source tokens per chunk. Chunking preserved paragraph boundaries: we split the source excerpt on blankline paragraph breaks, added whole paragraphs to the current chunk until adding the next paragraph would exceed the token limit, and then started new chunk. If single paragraph exceeded the limit, it was kept intact as an oversized chunk rather than split mid-paragraph. Each chunk was translated independently with the same literarytranslation prompt used in P1. The chunk translations were then concatenated in source order with paragraph breaks preserved, producing complete first-pass English draft. Finally, as in P1, the postediting stage received the complete source excerpt and the complete concatenated English draft, allowing the model to revise across chunk boundaries for consistency of voice, terminology, register, narrative perspective, and local fluency. [P3] Agentic pipeline. The entire pipeline is presented in Figure 2. We report the agent responsible for each task and the associated checkpoint in Table 6. The core parts of the pipelines are: (1) data preparation including style analysis, (2) chunklevel translation, and (3) excerpt-level review. For (1) and (2) we implement metrics and guard gates that send translation for edits if any issues are spotted. Below we explain the rubrics and acceptance gate mechanism. 19 [P3] Agent review rubrics and acceptance gates. The chunk-level acceptance gate combined two automatic reviews. First, the modified LiTransProQA review asked 25 yes/no/maybe questions about translation quality, such as faithfulness, cultural transfer, voice and more. chunk passed this qualityestimation component only if it received no NO judgments and at most five MAYBE judgments. Second, literary review checked whether the chunk preserved the sources accuracy, voice, dialogue, and prose, and recorded findings with LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH, or CRITICAL severity. chunk passed the literary component only if every lens passed and there were no MEDIUM+ findings.28 The local acceptance gate failed any chunk that failed either component and sent only those chunks to the next revision cycle. The excerpt-level final gate combined full-draft book review with cross-chunk audit. The book review targeted global translation defects such as repeated translationese, flattening, loss of ambiguity, etc., visible only when reading the excerpt as whole. The cross-chunk audit targeted boundary and consistency problems, such as inconsistent terms, narrator or register drift, seam issues, etc. Both reviews used the same four-level severity scale, and the final gate passed only when neither review found any MEDIUM+ issue; otherwise, affected chunks were sent to final revision. Pipeline selection. We translate 8K-word excerpts from 16 development books under all five setups  (Table 7)  .29 In blind five-way preference task, six raters (the authors and volunteer colleagues) ranked the outputs for all 16 development excerpts. The raters were asked to read at least 2K words to assure everyone reads past the chunk boundary. The raters can choose to read more. Besides choosing the best performing translation in their set of five candidates, each rater shares their observations on other translations. Overall, most outputs were comparable, except GPT-5.4 (P1) which noticeably underperformed30. We adopted the agentic pipeline (P3), which was preferred by small margin owing to its more idiomatic vocabu28Following Pham et al. (2026) we denote MEDIUM+ as any issue of severity medium or higher that would usually require revision. This excludes small issues marked as LOW. 29Because agents make many model calls, running them via API is prohibitively expensive; instead we used subscription services, which cap our translation count. 30P3 (5/16), P1 with Gemini 3.1 Pro (4/16), P2 with Gemini 3.1 Pro (3/16), P2 with GPT-5.4 (3/16), and P1 with GPT5.4 (1/16). lary. See below for the prompts and pipeline details used for this process.

sections.title: C Agentic pipeline runs
sections.content: In this section of the appendix we provide more details on the agentic pipeline runs and artifacts it produced. All artifacts from agentic runs are shared at github.com/Yves575/lait. Runs summary. We report the details of all runs in Table 8, including the number of chunks and excerpts which pass acceptance gate within the enforced limits. We note that even when the final translation failed to pass within the enforced limits we observe that increasing the limits did not show justifiable improvements, while the failed translation was often acceptable. Artifact footprint. The selected agentic translation pipeline outputs comprise 35 completed runs: 15 evaluation-book runs, 16 developmentbook runs, and 4 multilingual target-language runs. Across these runs, the pipeline processed 441 source-language chunks and produced 1,059 chunkcycle translation or revision artifacts, counting each completed translation or revision cycle for chunk once. Job-level logs were available for 31 runs and record 2,930 agent jobs, including 19 failed jobs; four development-book runs were completed but untracked in the job-metric logs. At the automatic final acceptance gate, 13 runs passed and 22 retained flagged issues, with 74 chunks flagged in total  (Table 9)  .

sections.title: D Human evaluation
sections.content: In this section of the appendix, we add more details about our human evaluation protocol, including participant recruitment, participant profiles, guidelines, and interface screenshots. Participant recruitment. The participants for the experiments were recruited using freelancing platform Upwork. We limit our search to avid readers, who read at least one book in English per month and have native-level command of the language. We asked them to complete an entry questionnaire  (Table 15)  . We report their demographics in Table 10 and AI use frequency in Table 11. Procedure. Before the experiment all participants were asked to sign the consent form (see Table 12, Table 13 and Table 14). They were also asked to fill in demographic survey  (Table 15)  . 20 All surveys were administered through Microsoft Forms. During the recruitment stage, we additionally ask them to report their reading habits. Questions. During the human evaluation, the readers fill in three types of questionnaires embedded in the interface. In the immersive reading they are asked to fill in questionnaire about each text separately (single-reading; Table 16), and then about both texts (comparison; Table 17). In the close reading each chunk pair is accompanied by short questionnaire asking them to compare both translations  (Table 18)  . Interface. Figure 17 shows the immersive reading interface where participants read and evaluated HT and MT texts in isolation, with reading order being counterbalanced across all participants. Figure 18a shows the comparison interface where participants answered questions about the holistic differences between the two translations. Figure 18b shows the close reading interface where readers evaluated chunks of the two translations side-by-side to detect local differences; display order was randomized for each chunk pair. The full participant-facing guidelines are shown in Figure 14. Display normalization. We applied lightweight display normalization to reduce typographic inconsistencies across texts, including normalizing quotation marks, guillemets, apostrophes, and ellipses. This normalization was used for presentation and annotation consistency and was applied directly in the evaluation interface without affecting the source files. The same displayed text representation was used when recording span highlights, so participant offsets refer to the normalized interface text.

sections.title: E Human evaluation results
sections.content: In this section of the appendix, we report additional results and descriptive statistics of the human evaluation data. Overall results. Figure 21 gives the corresponding book-level preference results. Figure 26 shows the chunk-level preference matrix, and Figure 34 summarizes the same close-reading preferences by source language. Figure 20 and Figure 19 provide additional source-language breakdowns of highlighted span counts and span lengths. Agreement. We report inter-reader agreement at three levels: (1) single-reading ratings, (2) binary preference and origin judgments (MT or HT), and (3) span highlights. For the singlereading 5-point ratings, we report Gwets AC2 (Gwet, 2021) for acceptability, smoothness, immersiveness, would continue  (Table 20)  as it is more fit for skewed distribution data than Krippendorffs alpha and handles ordinal variables. For binary judgments, we report the Gwets AC1 (nominal data; Table 21) for excerpt-level MT identification, excerpt-level preference, and chunk-level preference. Chunk-level agreement is reported for all chunks, for chunks excluding similar quality judgments, and for the subset where both readers judged one translation as significantly better. We also report chunk-level agreement measured separately by book  (Table 22)  , showing how it varies across the items and after excluding similar quality judgments. For span highlights, we report overlap diagnostics rather than chance-corrected coefficient. span is counted as overlapping when it intersects span from the other reader on the same translation version; label-aware overlap additionally requires the same good/poor label. Overall span overlap was 0.307, and label-aware overlap was 0.217. Readers who agreed on the preferred translation overlapped more in their highlighted evidence (0.322 span overlap; 0.263 label-aware overlap) than readers who disagreed (0.280 span overlap; 0.137 label-aware overlap). Comment coding. We conduct inductive coding and develop schema to help us quantitatively analyze readers comments. The category labels for translation quality and the reasons for judging translation as AI-generated are described in Table 23 and Table 24 respectively. We present comment examples in Table 34. Comment analysis. Table 28 shows the top positive labels assigned to comments by readers who preferred HT in the immersive reading comparative questionnaire to indicate why they preferred it. Table 27 shows the top positive labels for com31HT = human-translated; MT = machine-translated; = paired ratings. AC2 colors follow Landis and Koch heuristic bands: red = slight, yellow = fair, green = moderate, blue = substantial, purple = almost perfect. 32n is paired judgments. Both HT, Both MT, and Diff. count paired annotator outcomes. For excerpt AI/MT identification, HT and MT refer to which version readers guessed was MT. AC1 colors follow the same heuristic bands as Table 20. 21 ments from readers who preferred MT in that questionnaire. Table 26 shows the top positive labels assigned to comments by readers who preferred HT in the close reading per-chunk questionnaire to indicate why they preferred it. Table 25 shows the top positive labels for comments from readers who preferred MT in that questionnaire. We further report tendencies mentioned in comments in Figure 23, which summarizes positive and negative aspects readers mentioned for MT and HT in the comparison comments. Similarly, Figure 24 shows the positive reasons readers gave for preferring HT or MT, separated by excerpt-level and chunk-level comparisons. As readers often mention not only positive things about their chosen translation but also negative things about the other candidate, we aim at visualizing these in Figure 25. Finally, we present book-level view of AI guesses per language (Figure 22), while Figure 27 shows book-level HT/MT preference direction and strength for each participant. Single-reading ratings. Figure 29 shows singlereading ratings for acceptability, smoothness, immersiveness, and would continue (reading), divided by the presentation order (i.e., ratings for what the readers saw first vs. second). We further report these ratings by individual preferences, indicating both the translation order and the readers final preference for MT vs. HT in the excerpt-level evaluation (Figure 28). AI-detection guesses. For AI-detection questions, we compare readers single-reading guesses with their final comparison guesses. Additional descriptive flows for guess correctness and confidence are shown in Figure 30a and Figure 30b. Readers who often use AI were NOT better at detecting AI translation. Since we collect readers AI experience we check whether the more experienced readers were better at detecting AI translation. Overall, we do not see any apparent trends or correlation between the AI experience and detection accuracy  (Table 19)  . Good and poor span-level highlights. In the close reading, participants highlight spans they perceive as well and poorly worded. Post-study interviews. Participants were asked to answer several questions about their opinions on LLM translations before and after the study. The questions can be found in Table 29. We report readers post-study responses across four tables: Table 30, Table 31, Table 32 and Table 33. The tables are split by participant group for readability. Participants were initially skeptical about the quality of LLM translation: some, who have previously edited AI translations, recall them being of poor quality, and thus adjust their expectations accordingly, while other non-experts did not trust LLMs to produce good narrative from anecdotal experience. However, most participants, even those who correctly discriminated HT from MT, were pleasantly surprised by MTs quality, despite their general preference towards HT, one participant even preferred MT over HT despite correctly identifying them! Furthermore, several participants noted that the LLM translations did not contain the usual cues that they typically associate with LLM translations and writing: one participant states that they would have no inkling of it being translated by AI if it was surrounded by human-written books in their personal book collection. This view is not universal among participants, however. Some state that despite MT texts being semantically accurate, which is the goal of general-purpose translation system, they write too literally, frequently miss subtle context cues in the story, mistranslate idioms or other culturallysensitive phrases, and lack the stylistic and narrative requirements of fiction, [MT] tends to flatten the authors voice. Conversely, many participants were disappointed in the quality of HT excerpts of some of the books, either due to mistakes that should have been caught during editing or overall poor quality. The participants general consensus was that agentic LLM translation systems are not ready for standalone publication, but may be integrated to generate first drafts to be further edited by expert human translators. # 1 2 3 Question What were your expectations for the quality of AI translation before starting this experiment? You {correctly/incorrectly} identified the AI-translated version of Book 1, and {correctly/incorrectly} identified the AI-translated version of Book 2. Are you surprised by your accuracy? What do you think about the quality of AI translation now, after reading the translation? Any other comments? Table 29: Post-study questionnaire. All responses are free text.

sections.title: F Case study results
sections.content: In this section of the appendix, we detail our case study targeting translation into languages other than English, specifically Spanish, French, Polish, and Japanese. MT identification. Readers reliably identified MT for Polish (two raters), Spanish, and Japanese in the final excerpt-level comparison, while the French reader was incorrect (Figure 33). We also note that the Spanish reader was initially incorrect, identifying MT as HT when they first encountered it in single-reading (Figure 31), but they were able to reliably distinguish both during comparison, suggesting that perhaps the quality of translation into French and Spanish was higher than into Polish and Japanese. Preference judgments. Preference judgments moved strongly toward HT once readers compared the translations directly and then evaluated aligned chunks, with HT dominating both book-level preferences (4 out of 5 readers) and close-reading judgments (all 5 readers; Figure 21 and Figure 35) across target languages (Figure 32). Span annotations. Across target languages, readers marked mostly positive evidence in HT and mostly negative evidence in MT (Figure 20), with median span lengths reported to indicate how localized these judgments were (Figure 19).

sections.title: G Statistical analysis
sections.content: In this section of the appendix, we report details on the statistical models used for the quantitative results. The statistical models were chosen based on the type of response variable and data distribution. All analyses were conducted in using lme4 for mixed-effects models, lmerTest for inference in linear mixed-effects models, emmeans for order-adjusted marginal means and contrasts, and ordinal for cumulative-link models. The exact formulas and outputs are provided in Table 35 for preference judgments, Table 36 for span highlight, Table 37 for Likert-type ratings, and Table 38 for AI detection. Binary preferences. For binary choices (HT vs MT), we use logistic generalized linear mixedeffects models (GLMMs), with HT coded as 1 and MT as 0. These models estimate whether readers are more likely to choose HT than MT. When estimable, we include random intercepts for reader and book to account for repeated judgments by the same readers and repeated judgments of the same books. For dialogue and word-choice comparisons, responses had three levels: HT, MT, and no meaningful difference. We coded these as signed attribute preferences, with HT = +1, no difference = 0, and MT = -1. Crossed mixed-effects models with reader and book random intercepts were singular, hence we report order-adjusted fixed-effect linear models. Preference strength. For preference strength, we convert choices into signed numeric scores. HT preferences are coded as positive and MT preferences as negative, with larger absolute values indicating stronger preferences. We analyze these signed scores with linear mixed-effects models when the random-effects structure is estimable (random intercepts for reader and book). to Single-reading ratings. For 5-point Likert ratings, such as acceptability, smoothness, continue immersion, and willingness reading, we use cumulative-link mixed models with random intercepts for reader and book when estimable. These models are designed for ordered categorical responses, where the difference between adjacent scale points should not be treated as necessarily equal. AI detection. For AI-detection questions, responses were coded as binary correctness outcomes with 1 if the reader correctly identified the translation origin and 0 otherwise. In the singlereading task, MT excerpts were counted as correct when labeled machine-translated, and HT excerpts when labeled human-translated. We analyzed these responses with logistic generalized linear mixed-effects models, including random intercepts for reader and book when estimable. In the direct-comparison task, correctness meant selecting the actual MT version as more likely AItranslated; the crossed mixed-effects model was singular, so we report the corresponding intercept-only logistic model and an exact binomial test against chance. Confidence ratings were analyzed with linear mixed-effects models, restricted to correct guesses when testing whether correct responses were more confident after direct comparison. Highlight counts. For highlighted evidence spans, we use negative-binomial mixed-effects count models. These models are appropriate for 23 Paragraph-level metrics. On the aligned paragraph pairs, we run two metric families: COMET and METRICX. For each family, we use one reference-based metric and one reference-free quality-estimation metric: COMET-22 and METRICX use the professional HT as reference, while COMETKIWI (Rei et al., 2022) and METRICXQE (Juraska et al., 2024) use only the source and candidate translation. COMET scores are higheris-better, whereas METRICX scores are lower-isbetter. The metric input limits are 512 tokens for the COMET family and 1,536 tokens for the METRICX family. For reference-based metrics, this budget includes the source, candidate translation, and reference. For reference-free metrics, it only includes the source and candidate translation. Paragraph pairs that exceed the corresponding model limit are skipped for that metric. Reference-based metrics are not reported for the HT row, since this would score the reference against itself. For each book and system, we report the mean of all remaining paragraph scores for each metric. Paragraph-level par3 automatic-evaluation results for all systems are summarized in Table 39. Chunk-level comparison with human judgments. We evaluate the same approximately 300word aligned chunks used in the chunk-level close reading task. We score the HT and agentic/P3 MT chunks directly with METRICX-QE and with LITRANSPROQA (Zhang et al., 2025b), using Gemini 3.1 Pro as the question-answering judge (Google DeepMind, 2026). In addition, we map the paragraph-level COMETKIWI and METRICXQE scores onto the close-reading chunks. This mapping matches paragraph-level source text to the chunk source text in order and averages all paragraph scores assigned to chunk. When both HT and MT have paragraph scores for the same metric, we average over the shared paragraph indices so that the two systems are compared on the same source material. For every metric, we compute an oriented HTMT delta: positive values favor HT, and negative values favor the agentic/P3 MT. Chunk-level HTMT metric deltas used in the human-judgment comparison are summarized in Table 40. count data when the variance is larger than expected under simple Poisson model. Random effects. Across mixed-effects analyses, we include random intercepts for reader and book whenever estimable (i.e., does not result in singular fit). If mixed-effects model is singular, meaning that one or more random-effect variances are estimated as zero or near-zero, we report the corresponding simpler model.

sections.title: H Automatic Evaluation
sections.content: In this section of the appendix, we report additional information on automatic evaluation. Evaluation process. We conduct two automatic evaluations. First, we compare the automatic metric scores of the five MT setups described in B; i.e., P1 and P2 with GPT-5.4, P1 and P2 with Gemini 3.1 Pro, and the agentic P3 pipeline. This comparison covers the 16-book development set and the 15-book evaluation set. Second, we evaluate whether automatic metrics recover readers preferences by comparing only the professional HT and the selected agentic (P3) MT pipeline on the same aligned close-reading chunks shown to participants in the human evaluation. Paragraph-level alignment. Because several automatic metrics are constrained by context-window size, we evaluate the translations on shorter aligned paragraph units rather than complete 8K-word excerpts or even 300-word chunks. We follow the par3 alignment procedure (Thai et al., 2022). For each excerpt, source paragraphs are paired with paragraph-level Microsoft Translate output, which serves as an English pivot. Each candidate English version, including professional HT and each MT system output, is sentence-aligned to this pivot. The candidate sentences aligned to each pivot paragraph are then grouped under the original source paragraph boundaries. After alignment, we compute diagnostics for each recovered paragraph pair and manually inspect flagged cases. We flag length issue when the larger paragraph is more than three times longer than the smaller paragraph, ignoring pairs where both sides are shorter than 10 characters. We also flag lexical-overlap issues when candidate paragraph has pairwise BLEU score below 1.5, against majority of the other MT outputs aligned to the same source paragraph. Orange bars show HT identified as MT, green bars show MT identified as MT, and darker shades indicate higher confidence. 33 Consent Form Consent Form - Evaluating machine translation of literary texts (Study #[REDACTED]) Thank you for your interest in our study! Who is conducting the study? The co-principal investigators (co-PIs) are [REDACTED] and [REDACTED]. Another researcher involved in data collection is [REDACTED], student at [REDACTED]. Contact: [REDACTED] or [REDACTED]. You can also reach out to us via the Upwork messaging tool. Who is funding this study? [REDACTED] awarded to [REDACTED]. Why are we doing this study? We want to investigate the quality of literary machine translation (i.e., Can AI translate literary texts as well as humans) Who can participate in this study? Participants are adults who are comfortable reading long literary texts in English (up to 8,000 words) and who read fiction in English regularly. Your participation is voluntary. You have the right to refuse to participate in this study. Refusal to participate or withdrawal/dropout after agreeing to participate and signing this consent form will not have any adverse effects or consequences. How is the study done? This study takes place online and will last about 4 to 4.5 hours, divided into 2 sessions over period of few days. If you agree to take part: (1) You will first read this consent form and indicate whether you agree to participate. (2) You will use your own device to complete the study online. personal computer is recommended. (3) You will complete short questionnaire about your demographic, language background, and reading habits (5min). In Part 1 (about 1.5 hours), you will read long excerpt from novel in translation (8,000 words in English), answer small number of questions about it, provide written comment, and indicate whether you think it was translated by person or produced using artificial intelligence. You will then read second translation of the same excerpt and complete the same set of questions. At the end of Part 1, you will choose which version you preferred overall and explain why. You are encouraged to take break between the readings. In Part 2 (about 2.5 hours), which will take place few days later, you will compare the two translations side by side in shorter chunks. For each chunk, you will highlight good and bad phrasing, choose the version you prefer, briefly explain why, and indicate whether the choice felt easy or difficult. You may take breaks during the study whenever needed. We may ask you for additional clarification if your comments are unclear. Table 12: Consent form presented to the participants prior to the evaluation. Consent Form (continued) Risks, benefits, and your privacy What are the risks of participating? This study poses minimal risk: none of the tasks involved in the experiment will have any direct or lasting impacts on participants. Possible inconveniences include fatigue or boredom, and we encourage taking breaks to prevent these. What are the benefits of participating? We do not think that taking part in this study will have direct benefits for you. However, in the future, others may benefit from what we learn from this study. Specifically, we think that this study might help us understand more about the quality of translation and how automatic machine translation works. Will you be paid for taking part in this research study? You will receive payment of $110 USD for the entire task (part 1 and part 2). You will receive the money within one week of submitting your answers via the Upwork payment system. If you withdraw before completing the study, compensation will be prorated based on the amount of the study completed. How will your privacy be maintained? Your confidentiality will be respected. Information that discloses your identity will not be released without your consent. All participants will be identified only by unique code number. Participants will not be identified by name in any reports of the completed study. Name will be collected and stored by the PIs in separate spreadsheet, and only for the purposes of matching name and code in case you decide to withdraw. The study involves questionnaire. The initial demographic questionnaire will be collected via Microsoft Forms, which stores data on Canadian servers. This data will be stored in secure [REDACTED]-hosted server to ensure your information remains within our local, protected environment. Consent forms and study responses will initially be collected through secure online tools used for this study. After collection, the data will be downloaded and stored on password-protected [REDACTED] researchers computer. Access to study data (identifiable only through participant codes) will be limited to the PI and research assistants, all of whom have completed the required ethics training. What if you decide to withdraw your consent to participate? You may withdraw from this study at any time without giving any reasons, up to two weeks from the date next to your signature in the consent form. If you choose to enter the study and withdraw later, all data collected about you will be destroyed. Please contact us through email ([REDACTED] or [REDACTED]) and state that you wish to withdraw your data from this study. Please include the date of your participation. After two weeks have passed from the date of collection, we will not be able to accommodate withdrawal request, as study data will already have been used in presentations and publications. Results, contact information, and future data usage Study results. The main findings will be reported in academic presentations and publications. Contact for Complaints. If you have any concerns about your rights as research participant and/or your experiences while participating in this study, please contact the Director, Research Ethics at [REDACTED] or [REDACTED]. Future use of participant data. The data will be coded and stored on [REDACTED] servers. No identifying information will be shared outside of our research group. All data that we collect will be used for research purposes only. De-identified study data will be made available to other researchers for future, unspecified use in academic publications and open-access servers. While limited study data will be accessible to others once published (e.g., age, gender, language background), we will never share identifying or confidential information. Your continuation on the online experiment platform indicates that you received copy of this consent form for your own records and that you consent to participate in the study.
