Whether the choice of specific words or phrases is natural in English at the local level. Covers words/phrases that are grammatically correct and even understandable, but sound off (e.g., awkward) in English. Correct use of English idioms, set phrases, conventional collocations, and figure-of-speech precision; the negative side covers both misused/garbled idioms and over-reliance on clichéd or trite expressions. Whether word choices match the context in aspects such as formality, period (e.g., historical vs contemporary), dialect/local speech, genre (e.g., sci-fi), speaker/situation (e.g., emotional context). Lexical interest of the vocabulary used, such as unusual, varied, atmospheric words and turns of phrase vs. generic, common, sterile, dumbed-down choices. Independent of B8 (a passage can be rich-and-concise or rich-and-wordy). Translators localization decisions in both directions: localizing/explicating foreign concepts for the target reader (e.g., glossing terms, Americanizing place names) vs. preserving foreignness (leaving names/terms in source language, retaining cultural markers). Sentence length, complexity, word order, run-ons, fragments, choppiness, and variety vs. monotony across sentences. Whether the translation remains the same choices across the text in terms of vocabulary, voice, register, etc. (e.g., whether character names are translated consistently). The texts visual and typographic conventions, such as the typesetter-and-stylistic-choice layer that sits between grammar and content. It includes paragraph structure (breaks, dividers), dialogue formatting (quotation vs em-dashes). Overuse of the same words, phrases, structures whether variation or compression would be better. This includes unnecessary lexical repetition, redundant content, etc. Source material (words or phrases) that appear untranslated in the target text and are perceived by the reader as translation failures not deliberate choices to retain the source word for culture preservation. Whether the dialogue sounds like how people actually speak in the specific situation, context, emotional moment. It covers cases such as word plausability in speech (of this character), belivable phrasing etc. Whether characters come through as distinct, identifiable, and well-matched to who they are. B2 evaluates the voice of characters and narrators: both in dialogue and in prose attributed to characters perspective. character distinctness, attribution & character fit Whether the reader can picture the scene and whether word choices conjure clear mental images even when both options are technically correct; includes the craft distinction of showing vs. telling (scene-rendering that creates imagery vs. bare informational delivery). Use and effectiveness of metaphors, similes, imagery, and atmospheric or evocative phrasings; fires positively for evocative figures and negatively for misfiring ones that dont match the referent. Effectiveness of conveying the characters or scenes emotions to the reader (distinct from C4: B5 is about how well the reader can feel the storys emotional elements, while C4 is about whether the text itself feels alive or soulful). Story structure: scene transitions, paragraph / section flow, balance of exposition vs. action, story cohesion / motif recall (themes and descriptions that call back to previous text in the story). Pol. +/ +/ +/ +/ +/ +/ +/ +/ +/ +/ +/ +/ +/ +/ +/ +/ thoughts into narration), narrators distance from the action, narrator addressing the reader directly; point-of-view handling like POV shifts. Narrative-level pacing: info-dumps, lengthy descriptions that interrupt action; phrase-level wordiness (e.g. one word vs. whole phrase to describe the same thing). Independent of A6. Whether the reader could understand what was happening: local sentence-level understanding and global scene/plot-level comprehension (these can fire independently). Whether the prose flowed or the reader kept getting stuck; i.e. how much friction was in the reading experience, separate from whether they understood it. Whether the reader was pulled into the story: being in the world, losing awareness of reading, focused attention on the scene. Holistic judgement on whether the prose feels alive and human vs. robotic or mechanical (distinct from A3: individual word choices or sentences can feel natural, while the whole story can overall feel soulless). General reader satisfaction with the text: liked it, pleasant to read, pleasure, enjoyed both; broader affective stance not related to specific dimension, can be used as catch-all for positive sentiment when no specific details given. Holistic judgment on whether the prose reads as if originally written in English vs. feels like its been translated. Whether the translator adapted to English idioms and general audience expectations (positive) vs. translated word-for-word from the source language (negative); can fire with D4 when literalness is interpreted as cue for MT. How well the text preserves the source meaning, voice, emotion, structure, subtle implications, connotations, and specific facts; usually perceived at the chunk-level, and often inferred by comparing small detail inconsistencies between two presented translations. Holistic reader feeling that the translation feels machine-generated or human, but without naming specific cues. Reader explicitly articulates the cue that triggers the MT/AI impression: e.g., em-dash overuse, bullet-point styling, would overuse, US English defaults, anthropomorphizing concepts, and other details that are perceived as tells for AI-generated text. +/ +/ +/ +/ +/ +/ +/ +/ +/ +/ Narration & POV effects Narrator stance: interior monologue, free indirect discourse (blending characters +/ D4 MT/AI tell named Table 23: Annotation scheme for participant comments on Single Q5, Q6, Compar. Q4, and Chunk Justif. Only positive codes are applied to comments for Single Q5, and only negative codes are applied for Single Q6. For Compar. Q4 and Chunk Justif., positive codes are applied to match the comments positive aspects for the preferred translation, and negative codes applied to match the negative aspects of the non-preferred translation. Code Aspect Definition (AI-pointing) M1 M2 M4 M5 M6 M7 Literalism / source-bleed Word choice Sentence flow, structure & grammar Formatting & surface artifacts Wrong tone or mixed register Translation feels word-for-word or dictionary-like; source-language structure, idiom, or convention bleeds through; the method feels direct and unedited rather than adapted for English. specific word or short phrase reads as off; wrong, stilted, archaic, jargon, bland, generic, or conversely vivid and colloquial. The reader points at the word itself. How sentences are shaped and how they connect: choppy, truncated, run-on, abrupt, copy-paste structure, sentences that do not link, and grammatical mechanics such as tense inconsistency, verb agreement errors, modal misuse, or overuse of grammatical forms. Visible page-level mechanics not folk-theory framed: punctuation, scene dividers, quotation marks, dialogue formatting, and encoding glitches. Em-dashes as an AI sign are coded as M10. Whole passage is in wrong tone for novel, such as manual, textbook, technical, or Wikipedia-mode prose, or register switches and mixes inside the passage, such as US/UK slang together, source-culture and target-culture mixing, or formal-informal swings. Text uses more words than needed: padding, extra words, info-dumping, or lack of economy. Different from M5; wordiness can occur in any mode. Wordiness / over-explaining Voice & emotional life Whether prose is alive, e.g., engages, immerses, evokes mood, conveys emotion, Meaning errors M9 M10 Comprehension difficulty Folk theory invoked gives characters distinct voices. Fires on flatness, mechanical feel, characters sounding alike, failure to evoke scene, inability to picture/feel. Immersion lives here. Text gets meaning, logic, embodied physics, or factual content wrong, e.g., impossible images, lost nuance, dumbed-down content. Error is in the text, not the readers parsing. Reader could not follow, had to reread, needed the other version, or was confused by specific passage. About reader experience, not text defect. Reader explicitly references what AI does, such as named stereotype, prior experience, or capability claim. Triggers on language like classic sign of AI, AI tends to, Id expect from AI, or beyond what AI is capable of. Human-pointing examples translated in way that was corrected to make sense for English in frenzy more accurate than with fever some things flow more smoothly in T1; more variation in sentence lengths slightly better formatting with proper nouns older style consistent with fantasy economical; tight prose more natural and conveys more emotion; made me feel like sassy idol T2 breathed through his mouth more logical; T2 treats speaking underwater as hypothetical rather than given T2 much easier to understand; version of the sentence clearer in T2 Human translators manage fluidity AI does not match; would not think AI capable of that leap Table 24: Annotation scheme for participant comments on Compar. sections.title: 10 D3. Faithfulness to original
sections.content: 35 Overall preferred the wording used in MT. HT felt slightly simple at times, e.g., being beaten with sticks felt like overexplaining. 29 slightly preferred the more formal/older English style of MT but both translations seemed to sufficiently convey the message. 26 MT was better translation as it was generally easier to read with better natural flow. 23 MT uses more interesting, unusual words like pellet of paper instead of ball of paper and loathsome instead of hideous. 22 only picked MT as my preference because the descriptions, like of their home, gave me fuller picture of the scene. 17 HT is all mixed up in the narrative and it is not as logically rolled out as in MT. There is significantly better flow in MT and it makes it more understandable and easier to follow. 17 MT has tighter feel that comes across as more of conversation than HT does. 16 overall liked the wording of MT better because it sounded to me more like how the character of Sarah, who is the wife of an author, might speak. 16 thought HT was referring to literal band of boys because of the instrument talk, so prefer MT because the word gang was much clearer. 13 MT has marginally better flow and leans more faithfully on the Japanese, which results in slightly awkward English reading experience, but it is not by any means bad. Table 25: Top Q5 POSITIVE labels assigned to MT in the chunk-level comparison task. Counts are response-level label presences.

sections.title: 10 A1. Grammar & spelling
sections.content: 92 HT simplified lot of overcomplicated sentences we see in MT, which makes text in HT flow much better. Sentences in MT are too long and overly complicated. 87 HT is more succinct, more precise, and more idiomatic. MT is unnecessarily wordy, e.g., frightened her terribly instead of terrified her. 80 HT has more natural phrasing while MT sounds more like literal translation. Better word choice makes HT easier to read and makes the text flow better when reading. 64 found myself having to reread sentences in MT to follow what it was saying. It was just far easier to understand in HT. 39 HT had better choice of splitting it up into smaller sentences which make it easier to read. 39 HT has much clearer narrative picture and reads beautifully. The descriptive passages hit very well and it is just very enjoyable to read. 31 HT has better narrative and conversational flow, as well as tighter descriptions and better arrangement of the action. 26 Some sentences in MT, while technically correct, are not how person would say it in English. Well be happy to comply, from HT is more fitting. 24 HT has more unusual and interesting word choice, such as omnipresent water instead of just all that water and sorrowful note appeared in his voice rather than sadness entered his voice. 22 HT has much more natural use of grammar and punctuation. struggled to understand how these two sentences are connected when initially read them in MT. sections.title: 10 A7. Cultural adaptation
sections.content: 5 MT has better flow and word choice overall compared to HT, which had me rereading sentences to grasp their meaning more than once. 5 The language in MT is much more natural than HT and is clearer to understand. MT pulls the reader in with more vivid descriptions and is generally well-written. HT was boring to read and felt my mind wandering. 5 enjoyed both translations immensely and found them easy to read. prefer MTs word choices because they come off as more intricate and varied to me. It is more formal than HTs phrasing in some parts, without feeling unnatural. 4 MT uses richer language with more unusual turns of phrase and wording. This creates better atmosphere and more interest. HT uses regular language that seems somehow sterile and more common. 4 MT made more of an effort to explain foreign words than HT, like the Pentateuch in HT is translated as the Five Books of Moses in MT. have no idea if that is an accurate translation, but it was easier for me to understand. 3 felt like was able to pick up the essence of the characters more in MT, for instance, the difference between Sarah thinking He melts me, the idiot and Im touched. MT just gave clearer picture of personalities and relationships with its words. 3 MT also made language choices, particularly the use of an older style of English, that are generally more consistent with this style of fantasy, e.g. [MT] Do not take offence vs. [HT] Dont be cross. 3 The language in MT is much more natural than HT and is clearer to understand. MT pulls the reader in with more vivid descriptions and is generally well-written. 3 When reading HT the last sentence confused me initially, But what if he didnt when he could see it, while in MT it seemed much clearer: But what did it matter that he did not know, when he saw. 2 MT made more of an effort to explain foreign words than HT, like the Pentateuch in HT is translated as the Five Books of Moses in MT. have no idea if that is an accurate translation, but it was easier for me to understand as someone with very little knowledge of Jewish teachings. Table 27: Top Q3 POSITIVE labels assigned to MT in the Part 1 comparison questionnaire. Counts are responselevel label presences.

sections.title: 10 D1. Doesn’t read as translated
sections.content: 15 HT was much easier for me to read than MT. The wording flowed better and made it seamless reading experience, whereas in MT felt like had to reread sentences now and then to make sure was understanding right. 8 HT was far easier to follow in terms of flow and word choice. MT had at times, bizarre punctuation choices and was very choppy. MT made it difficult to determine who was speaking. 6 The HT translation is simply more human. MT translation is overall proper with small errors, but there is no emotional impact on the reader. HT flows better, the sentences are more real for lack of better word. 6 The wording in both translations was good, but where HT surpassed MT was in sentence structure, avoiding too many long sentences, and formatting, dividers between scenes. 5 From the start of the story, HT was lot clearer to me and think lot of that has to do with punctuation and word choice. MT does not necessarily use obscure or uncommon words, but the translation does not feel quite right at times. 5 HT flowed better. Text was broken up and summarized. The dialogue tags were more natural. like the shorter sentences in the first translation. 4 HT had no formatting issues that noticed, for one, and also had richness/uniqueness to it that pulled me in. The dialogue flowed well and, while complicated at times, is not unusual for an epic fantasy book of this nature. 3 The descriptions were more 3 dimensional in HT. They interacted with one another and recalled past phrasing. They drew pictures more vividly and expressed thought in more cohesive manner. The flow had more nuanced cadence and was less flat than MT. 2 HT stands out to me as clearly more human translated. Translations, but also literary fiction in particular, can get away with bit of weird/removed style of writing that made MT not seem strange to me, but HT was more natural. 2 HT also felt more like reading story in English rather than translated story. MT definitely read like someone or something translating word by word; it did not feel natural while reading. Table 28: Top Q3 POSITIVE labels assigned to HT in the Part 1 comparison questionnaire. Counts are responselevel label presences. P1 Identified Book 1: yes Identified Book 2: no Expected worse: yes Q1 expected the AI translations to be more obviously written by AI. was impressed even with the translations didnt prefer. Q2 Im not that surprised actually, book 2 was by far harder to pick favorite of. had really hard time even finding flaws, and when did Id find them more on preference level and then both translations had some. Q3 think its so good, hence my mistaking it as human. think it read really smoothly and if it was one of my books, would have no inkling of it being translated by AI Q4 no other comments come to mind! Thanks for having it, it was so fun! P2 Identified Book 1: yes Identified Book 2: yes Expected worse: yes Q1 My expectations for the quality of AI translation before starting this experiment were that they would be little confusing to read because they would be directly translated, which sometimes does not mean translated correctly. It was my impression that usually with different languages, the English translations were not exactly correct. So figured there would be some awkward or incorrect wording. Q2 am not surprised by my accuracy in correctly identifying the AI translations of both Book 1 and Book 2. Q3 My opinion of AI and its quality is the same as it was before, which is that it can be helpful but at the end of the day only real human can get something to the way it needs to be. dont think AI has the ability to understand when something doesnt sound right or use context to correct something. real person, on the other hand, can take something translated poorly and rework it in order for it to make sense. Q4 N/A P3 Identified Book 1: no Identified Book 2: no Expected worse: yes Q1 expected the writing quality to be lot worse. thought that the resulting text would be more in line with that of AI-written text (not translated, entirely AI-generated from prompts). Q2 Not particularly. leaned towards one being AI over the other mainly because of quality differences. do still believe that with few copies of each, humans could learn to pick up the patterns and correctly detect which is AI-generated. Q3 Its substantially better than expected. definitely feel that its one of the best applications of LLMs that Ive seen. Q4 The quality of the human translations were quite bit worse than expected. Its almost like they didnt use an editor to review it afterwards. There were pretty clear issues that shouldnt have slipped through. think that version thats objectively higher quality than both could be made by combining the better parts of each translation. P4 Identified Book 1: no Identified Book 2: no Expected worse: yes Q1 My expectations of the AI translations were low, based on the poor quality of many of the AI-generated books edit and humanize. Q2 am somewhat surprised because was fairly sure. However, the quality was much better than what Im used to, and the books didnt have many of the usual tells often see. Q3 think it has improved remarkably in very short time. Q4 N/A Table 30: Post-study responses grouped by participant (part 1 of 4; participants P1P4). Q1 asks about expectations for AI translation quality before the study; Q2 asks whether participants were surprised by their AI-identification accuracy; Q3 asks for their post-study view of AI translation quality; Q4 collects other comments. 50 Identified Book 1: no Identified Book 2: no Expected worse: no Q1 expected them to be good. use ChatGPT multiple times day, for many reasons. lot of times its to get answers for random things that pop in my head that Im curious about, (that happens quite bit, maybe more than most people, my brain is funny like that). But also have been using it ton the past few months, daily, with my resume as Im job hunting. Ive seen AI give amazing responses, but Ive also seen it be incorrect and inconsistent. So while my expectations were high, had constant hesitation wondering how much faith wanted to put into the machine. If that makes sense. Q2 Im not surprised now that Ive completed them. For both books, when it came to Part 2 and was annotating them and comparing them, knew Id made the wrong choices. Reading them one at time and day apart made huge, huge difference and that surprised me. Q3 think its inconsistent, for starters. dont think its safe to hand projects over to and be confident that youll get flawless result. Ive often wondered about how machine can replace human in instances like this, when tone and even the smallest nuances, make the biggest differences. Theyre also so literal with the words, they cant grasp the context. think AI translations make for great rough draft. Maybe thats the best way to put it. Q4 Id be curious to know if my annotations, the translations that critiqued and disliked the most, were AI. When got to that point is where feel realized what was what. Or maybe just didnt care for how person translated them, and was critiquing them! Oh gosh.. haha! Identified Book 1: yes Identified Book 2: yes Expected worse: no Q1 expected AI to produce competent literal translation but to slip up with idiomatic language and polysemous words, and its translation of scenes that are supposed to evoke emotion. Q2 Not really surprised (but certainly gratified) because translating fiction is tricky even for humans. think it was with Henning Mankell that first started paying attention to the names of translators when one of the books in his Wallander series sounded off. And he used top-notch human translators! Q3 On the whole, assume that AI accurately reproduced the text from the original to English but it tends to flatten the authors voice. That is not problem when all the reader wants is functional translation of information. But that is not why we read fiction. Q4 An AI translation is still lot like CliffsNotes: function without form. doubt War and Peace will get an AI makeover any time soon! But shouldnt be too cock-a-hoop. AI learns 24/7 and will probably have to buy another hat to eat in the not too distant future. P7 Identified Book 1: no Identified Book 2: no Expected worse: yes Q1 My expectations were pretty low. was paid to clean up machine translation of non-fiction book about the outbreak of war in Ukraine in 2022 for Ibidem press, and it was pretty clunky. The idioms were very off. This was much better. Q2 am only little surprised by my accuracy, now that both tasks are finished. Q3 think that rather than demonstrating the quality of AI translation, this project demonstrated to me how challenging it is to judge the quality of any translation of piece of literature from the opening pages. The Japanese novel really seemed off to me in how it was depicting the protagonist she seemed much too squirrely to be successful businesswoman. attributed this to an AI-translator, but having looked up the book, now know she was supposed to seem squirrelly because she is stalker on the verge of giving in to obsession. After reading those texts, can definitely see the appeal of working with an AI-translator rather than human-translator for an author looking to truly preserve their voice in piece of fiction. The two tasks have not impacted how feel about AI writing at all. Q4 This project inspired me to dust off the now 15-year-old translation of Renaissance Latin text in the style of Sallust that was core component of my doctoral project and which, because the text seems just so unappealing to English readers, had not ever planned on returning to... and the LLMs analysis and discussion with me of the text confirmed that the text just isnt translateable today ... when no one wants to read Gibbons-esque prose. Thank you for this opportunity. It was fun task, and am enjoying reading Hooked so much that have sent copies to friends. Table 31: Post-study responses grouped by participant (part 2 of 4; participants P5P7). Questions are as in Table 30. 51 P8 Identified Book 1: yes Identified Book 2: yes Expected worse: yes Q1 had realistic expectation of accurate translations in terms of word choice, but wasnt sure about the sentence construction on grammar level. wasnt expecting 1v1 direct translation, because that doesnt work from Japanese to English, but thought there would be more. . . mechanical issues. Q2 am and Im not. think it comes down to the nature of the translation. Book 2 was much easier to detect AI on because the human-led translation was so tightly done on narrative level. It flowed very well, with some variance in terms of paragraph structures that attributed to the nature of the original language. Book One was hard to figure out, but chose the version did because of the work currently do. Most of my authors use AI to wholly or in part generate manuscript which they submit to me for humanizing. These manuscripts have certain linguistic conventions (truncated sentences, over-reliance on mechanical or stage direction language, certain buzzwords to describe human emotions: the architecture of face, etc.) that made me view the translations through that lens. Im trained to spot short, choppy sentences and odd word choices as being AI-generated. Q3 Im pleasantly surprised at the quality. Its not perfect, but its definitely not going to stop me from reading. Q4 think the style of the human translation played large part in how viewed the AI version. The translator who chose more Western style felt more natural to me, and that made it easier to pick out an AI version. Book Ones translator felt more faithful to the tone and style of the Japanese original, which read oddly to my English eyes. This was an honestly fascinating look at side of my industry that didnt have before. greatly appreciated this chance to be part of the project, and its solidified my desire to write down my observations on how AI handles fiction. P9 Identified Book 1: yes Identified Book 2: yes Expected worse: yes Q1 expected the translation to be lot worse than it actually was (almost like how Google Translate translates sentence - usually in way no English speaker would say it). Q2 Im not surprised by the accuracy - wasnt completely confident, of course, but didnt expect human to miss the obvious issues in the AI translations (dialogue issues and scene break/double hyphen issues). Q3 The issues in the AI translations were more to do with style than actual major wording problems, which think is amazing. Q4 think the books were translated very well in all versions, few easily-fixable issues aside. If AI could do the brunt work in translations, then human can just review it and fix those small issues (or train the model to fix it itself in the future!) P10 Identified Book 1: no Identified Book 2: yes Expected worse: yes Q1 My expectations for AI translation were pretty low coming into this experiment. have seen some pretty bad translations in the past. Q2 Im surprised! But do think Book 1s translation was lot better than Book 2s. It was easier in Book 2 to differentiate between the two because of the amount of dialogue, in my opinion, and the way things were phrased. Q3 think there is some merit to exploring AI-assisted translations, but think they need human revisions to streamline the overall translation. Q4 Thanks for giving me the opportunity to participate in the study! P11 Identified Book 1: yes Identified Book 2: no Expected worse: yes Q1 expected the AI translation to be obvious. read lot of AI generated work and it has very specific tells and often devolves into nonsense. Q2 am not surprised. Book 1 felt very obvious when comparing the two translations. Book 2 was much harder. Im not even sure which one chose because the translations were of similar quality in my opinion (both felt kind of off). Q3 was surprised by the quality of the translation in Book 1, it was much better than expected, but the human-generated translation was even better and provided context dont think an LLM is capable of. can see good use case for it. struggled little with both translations of Book2 so its hard to draw any conclusions there. Q4 As lover of books and languages really enjoyed doing this! Thank you for the experience. Oh! And Ill also add that if had only read T1 from book 1, would not have guessed it was AI translated. It was only obvious by comparison. was very suspicious of there being double blind on the second book [face with tears of joy emoji] Table 32: Post-study responses grouped by participant (part 3 of 4; participants P8P11). Questions are as in Table 30. 52 P12 Identified Book 1: yes Identified Book 2: yes Expected worse: yes Q1 think was expecting less than what read. As in, thought it would be easier to detect the AI. Q2 No, Im not surprised. do LOT of work with AI and there are some tells that give it away. There is also the conversational tone that AI hasnt quite nailed down. Q3 think the first book, the one about the spaceship, was much better. dont know if its the original author writing the book in more conversational tone, or if the AI chose better words. The Michealangelo book was extremely difficult to read. Both version were challenging, which tells me thats the way the author wrote it. Q4 N/A P13 Identified Book 1: yes Identified Book 2: no Expected worse: yes Q1 expected the quality of AI translation to be stilted and mechanical with the words not always in the right order. Q2 Yes, Im surprised by incorrectly identifying the AI-translation version of Book 2. Q3 still feel that the AI translation lacks human aspect, with less vivid descriptions. Q4 The human translation can vary depending on the experience/quality of the translator. human translator who is familiar with particular place or culture can bring their own human experience to the translation. P14 Identified Book 1: yes Identified Book 2: yes Expected worse: yes Q1 was expecting the quality to be somewhat worse than what read in the provided texts. More missing context due to language/cultural differences, more literal translations without replacing idioms, etc. Q2 thought it would be easier to identify the AI translation than it was in reality. When had two texts side by side it was quite easy, but when read them separately, it was quite similar to poorly done translation by human amateur. Q3 Both AI translations in the text were more or less acceptable from the point of view of literal translation. However, they were missing emotional import, poetic sense and failed to take into consideration that some things should be changed in translation based on the cultural and language differences in the output language. Q4 think AI can be useful tool if we are stuck and are trying to see how something can be said or written, but it should only be treated as tool or guide, there is no way that AI can properly convey everything - emotion, language style, etc, and take into consideration many, many things around the language that we do not think of but notice the difference when reading the text. P15 Identified Book 1: yes Identified Book 2: yes Expected worse: no Q1 expected grammatically correct translation of AI. It has become much better in the past few months and anticipated robust and accurate interpretation of the narrative. In the first book, one translation was significantly better than the other. In the second book, was disappointed by the grammar and use of words. There was lack of depth and engagement. Q2 am not surprised by my accuracy. read lot of AI and work with it every day. So can tell by grammar, use of colons and em dashes, and choice of words pretty clearly. Q3 think the translations need work. The first book was superior to the second book. The translations were poorly organized and lacked compelling narrative. Q4 It would be fairly easy to fix the translations so they dont sound so AI driven. HUmanizing does not take that much time. Please feel free to reach out if you need help with anything else. Table 33: Post-study responses grouped by participant (part 4 of 4; participants P12P15). Questions are as in Table 30.

The dialogue flowed well and, while complicated at times, is not unusual for an epic fantasy book of this nature. much preferred bento containers over plastic containers as it not only falls in line with the Japanese culture, it also doesnt sound cheap. The older-style language of MT better matches the period the story is set in. HT is little too modern, using words like glasses instead of spectacles and frames instead of rims. preferred the chief diggers dialogue in MT, it seemed to fit his character better. It also contrasted with the style of Preston Holt better, while in HT both characters had more similar dialogue patterns. could imagine the stories better in MT with more vivid language and liked the phrase windmill his arms which made the wording come alive. The dialogue in both feels clumsy in parts, especially the way Mona speaks, but Im guessing that might just be how the original book was written. lot of the sentences were far too long, and this, combined with word choice, made the paragraphs difficult to follow (this couldve just been poorly written original scene, though, and not necessarily poorly translated).
